turns.content: I'm trying to get back into Rust after five years, and I remember that threads are tricky. When I look at the rayon::ThreadPool documentation, it seems like it's just a way to manage how many threads we use for parallelism. But isn't there something more about scopes involved here? Like when I run code in a block, does that automatically limit which thread pool gets used?

That's a great starting point. When you create a rayon::ThreadPool with a custom size using the builder pattern, you're setting up a global resource for your program. However, if you want to isolate parallelism within a specific part of your code—say, just a few functions without affecting the rest—you would use a scoped pool instead. Think of it like this: a global pool is a big factory with many workers available everywhere in the building, whereas a scoped pool is like handing out a temporary badge to one department so they only use those specific workers for that shift.

Oh, I see. So if I just call some function inside rayon::scope, it automatically grabs from my global pool? And what happens if I don't have a global pool set up at all? Does the system just spawn threads on demand or does it fail?

If you don't create a global rayon::ThreadPool instance using the builder before importing your code, then calling functions that require a thread pool will result in an error because there's no active pool to borrow from. The library expects a valid pool to be present when you start using it for parallelism. You wouldn't just spawn threads on demand without first configuring how many should exist; you must explicitly define the number of available threads upfront.

Right, so I need to call rayon::ThreadPool::new() before doing anything else? But what if I want different parts of my code to run with different thread counts? Like some tasks needing 8 cores and others needing just 2?

Exactly. That's why you would use a scoped pool instead of relying on the global one. By creating a local scope around your function calls, you can specify exactly how many threads that particular section should utilize. For example, you might run one block with 8 threads and another separate block with only 2, ensuring each has independent control without interfering with the rest of your application's thread management.

That makes sense. I think I was confused earlier thinking scopes just inherit from a global setting automatically, but it seems like they need their own configuration. Is there a trait or method specifically for this isolation? Like does rayon::scope take an argument to set the count?

Yes. The key is using the rayon::scope function which allows you to create a local thread pool with your desired thread count. You pass the number of threads directly into that scope creation, and it establishes a temporary pool just for that duration. This gives you fine-grained control over parallelism without needing to manage multiple global pools or worry about one section hogging resources meant for another.

I am building a proof-of-concept to evaluate a custom thread pool using rayon::ThreadPool for scoped parallelism, but I keep seeing the global thread limit spike when my worker tasks try to spawn threads. Why does this happen and how do I actually control it?

The issue is that you are treating rayon::ThreadPool as if it automatically manages OS-level threads for every task, but the real reason the limit spikes is because each spawned thread creates its own stack and the global pool only tracks threads in the current scope. When you use a custom pool with rayon::scope, you must explicitly call .create() on your local thread pool before running tasks to ensure it doesn't pull from the global set.
That sounds too vague and I need concrete steps. If I use a custom pool with rayon::scope, do I just call .create() on every task or is there a different pattern?

You must instantiate your local thread pool once before the scope block and then pass it into the closure. The key distinction is that rayon::scope expects a thread pool object, so you create an instance of your custom pool type with a specific capacity limit before invoking .run() on tasks inside the scope.
I tried calling .create() directly in the closure but it still creates too many threads. Is there something wrong with how I am handling the thread pool object?

The problem is likely that you are creating a new thread pool instance for every iteration instead of reusing the same one across all tasks in your scope. You need to define your custom pool type once, then pass it into the closure so that all parallel work shares the same limited resource rather than each task spawning its own.
